metadata.title: Immigration Coverage in the Black Press and the General Audience Press: What Can Mixed Methods Reveal about Race and Immigration?

abstract: This paper has two aims. First, we apply Bourdieu's field theory to investigate media discourse on race and immigration, demonstrating how features of news organizations influence news content. Second, we compare contemporary natural language processing (NLP) techniques with qualitative hand-coding. Extending a previous study, we compare newspaper articles from the mainstream and black press in Atlanta. We find significant differences in both word-use and topical coverage in immigration articles aimed at the two audiences. With a focus on organizational resources and values, our quantitative approach to field theory facilitates a better understanding of the journalistic landscape.

paragraphs: Contemporary research on the news media, especially the way news media constructs, reproduces, and challenges social inequality, has followed two parallel tracks (Fraser 1990;Hall 1986;Nelson et al. 2018). In the area of media theory, the work of Habermas and Bourdieu have provided a theoretical framework to understand how media directed at different audiences proves vital for deliberative democracy, and how media organization, norms, and practices shape media content (Benson 2013;Benson and Neveu 2005;Benson and Saguy 2005;Marchetti 2005;Rohlinger 2007). 1 In the area of research methods, the growing number of automated content analysis techniques -or Natural Language Processing (NLP) -allow researchers to analyze thousands of news articles with content aimed at different audiences (DiMaggio, Nag, and Blei 2013;Nelson et al. 2018). In this paper, we bring these two tracks together by applying field theory to media coverage of immigration aimed at a mainstream and black audience, while also assessing the utility of NLP methods to compare these two corpora.

According to Bourdieu (1998Bourdieu ( , 2005)), journalism is a field with a distinct set of resources, practices, norms, and professional standards that shape content and that define what is considered quality news and what is considered dross (Benson 2006;Couldry 2003;Marchetti 2005). A field is "a structured space of positions in which the positions and their interrelations are determined by the distribution of different kinds of resources or 'capital'" (Thompson 1991, cited in Hesmondhalgh 2006:212). These fields -economic, educational, political, cultural, journalistic, etc. -are constructed through hierarchies of resources and positions (Benson and Neveu 2005;Garnham and Williams 1980;Meisenhelder 2000). The literature applying field theory to media coverage of immigration focuses on the generalist press within the U.S. or cross-nationally, and leave a consideration of "race" and audiences unexamined. One of the few studies that compare coverage of immigration in the mainstream and black press, Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) do not apply a field theory framework and rely on hand coding.

We contribute to these literatures by applying NLP techniques to study race and immigration in the journalistic field. We argue that differences in media coverage of immigration are tied to distinct racially-inflected logics in the mainstream and black press and to the critical perspectives of a black target audience. We build upon Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) and assess the utility of NLP to detect different logics in the black and general audience press by comparing NLP techniques with their original handcoding of the same corpus. As we show, NLP is especially adept at identifying multiple perspectives within a news source, and providing a statistical comparison of both the differences and similarities in media content across sources. We find that the NLP techniques verified the existence of a black counter-discourse in the black press -as seen through both significant differences in word-use and topical coverage -and identified two topics not found in hand-coding. However, NLP missed the critiques of racism found in the handcoding of black press articles.

Thus, this paper accomplishes two goals: 1) we demonstrate the utility of using Bourdieu's field theory to compare media sources that target two audiences differentiated by race and 2) we assess the value of applying NLP to a comparison of two different media sources. Through meeting these goals, we illuminate what field theory and mixed methods can teach us about race and immigration.

We proceed by discussing Bourdieu's field theory and move onto the scholarship using NLP techniques to study race and immigration. After providing an in-depth discussion of Browne et al.'s methods and findings, we describe the NLP methods that we use to analyze their corpus. We compare the NLP results with Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez's (2016) hand-coding results, discussing the relative benefits and drawbacks of each method. We offer specific tools through which NLP and qualitative analyses can be used as a "check" on each other. Applying field theory, we demonstrate the advantages of a theoretically-driven mixed-methods approach to text analysis.

sections.paper_id: Browne et al. - 2022 - Immigration Coverage in the Black Press and the General Audience Press What Can Mixed Methods Revea
paragraphs: Scholars have emphasized the necessity of taking a comparative approach to study the journalistic field, often contrasting media production in different geographies, with different intended audiences, or different organizational structures (Benson and Saguy 2005;Marchetti 2005). For instance, Benson (2013) compares coverage of immigration in French and U.S. general audience newspapers, finding that French publications are more critical of immigration policy, despite their higher reliance on state subsidies (e.g., the political field). He attributes these national differences to features of the journalistic fields in which the outlets operate, citing differing norms for reporting the news. French publications favor a "polemical debate" while U.S. publications tend to follow a "dramatic narrative." While Benson's (2013) comparison of media in the U.S. and France focuses on national differences in two "generalist" outlets aimed at a mainstream audience, other research on the journalistic field has compared generalist and "specialist" media outlets. On one hand, generalist and specialist outlets share certain norms: they expect "quality reporting" that meets a tight deadline, they strive to be the first outlet to report a story, and they are under constant pressure to attract advertising revenue and expand their audience (Benson 2013;Champagne 2005;Rohlinger et al. 2012;Schultz 2007). This presents conditions for similar issue-framing across generalist and specialist media. On the other hand, specialist outlets have their own goals and logics in the journalistic field that differentiate them from generalist outlets and render them a "sub-field" of journalism (Benson and Saguy 2005;Marchetti 2005). These distinct logics are tied to the characteristics of the target audience(s) as well as the goals, norms, resources, and organizational features of the specialist outlets (Marchetti 2005;Rohlinger et al. 2012).

Scholars applying Bourdieu's theory to the study of immigration have focused on comparing generalist outlets with partisan media as the "specialist" outlets (e.g., liberal/ left and conservative/right publications) (Benson 2013). They assert that "[p]artisan outlets target audiences with ideological interpretations of -and political perspectives on -the news. They may therefore cover different ideas and actors than those featured in the mainstream news" (Rohlinger et al. 2012:53). That is, differences in news coverage emerge from both the divergent audiences and the different norms and goals in generalist and partisan media. Rohlinger (2007Rohlinger ( , 2012) ) show how profitability is a constant pressure on the generalist press, which slants news coverage toward "news-grabbing" debates and political positions. While generalist media move toward building consensus, partisan outlets play an important role in a deliberative democracy, as they provide group-centered voices and perspectives not covered in the generalist press (Habermas 1996;Rohlinger 2007).

From the perspective of field theory, we contend that the history of racism and black exclusion from mainstream news sources represents a unique set of structural conditions shaping the field of news production and influencing news content. The black press follows a mission to promote civil rights and provide a counter-narrative on various topics (including immigration) (Pride and Wilson 1997). For instance, as the publisher of the Orlando Advocate, Kevin Seraaj stated, "I think we also have an obligation to have an editorial voice that speaks to matters in a way that perhaps mainstream media will never speak to them" (cited in Mangun and Perry 2020:306, emphasis added). The logic of the black press contains a mission that the generalist press does not traditionally shareengaging in political activism. For example, during the Civil Rights movement, the black press encouraged black readers to register to vote and to cast a ballot (Mangun and Perry 2020).

As Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) emphasize, the unique structural conditions in the black press generate news content that is distinct from the mainstream pressa critical "black counter-discourse." Black counter-discourses place black actors and issues at the center of the story within a critique of white racism (Dawson 1994). We contend that the concept of "black counter-discourses" can be incorporated into field theory as the expected "news content" within the black specialist press.

The theory of a black counter-discourse assumes that racism affects content in the mainstream press as well as the black press. Public sphere discourse in the mainstream press does not just sideline minority voices -it represents racial/ethnic minority groups in ways that reproduce the U.S. racial hierarchy (Feagin 2013;Fraser 1990). These frames draw boundaries between the "deserving" and "undeserving" of public benefits, associating the "undeserving" with specific racial groups (Brown 2013).

What Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez's (2016) articulation of the concept of black counter-discourse ignores -and what field theory contributes -is a consideration of the structural conditions that simultaneously produce similarities and differences in content. As we show, NLP provides a set of tools to quantify the similarities and differences in news coverage of policy issues, such as immigration, as predicted by field theory.

To our knowledge, our study is the first applying field theory to a comparison of discourse on immigration in the mainstream and the black press. The topic of immigration provides strategic leverage to compare both presses using field theory, since both these media sources cover immigration for a U.S.-born audience and therefore leave the positioning of their respective readership vis-a-vis a third group -immigrants -open to multiple framings and political stances (Abascal 2015). These multiple possibilities arise both from the norms in the journalistic field to present two sides of an issue, and the respective position of whites, blacks, and immigrants in the U.S. status hierarchy (Schultz 2007). In particular, black readers can align with white readers over their shared citizenship status to distance themselves from immigrants, or African-American readers can align with immigrants over their status as marginalized groups in the U.S. racial hierarchy to distance themselves from (non-Hispanic) whites (Abascal 2015).

sections.paper_id: Browne et al. - 2022 - Immigration Coverage in the Black Press and the General Audience Press What Can Mixed Methods Revea
paragraphs: Debates over the merits of qualitative and quantitative methodologies are a central part of social science. The proliferation of digitized text in the last twenty years and the increasing availability of sophisticated methods for NLP have forced researchers to consider these questions anew (Bail 2014;Evans and Aceves 2016). Importantly, recent analyses of text data using mixed methods approaches outline both the benefits and respective strengths of using qualitative and NLP methods (Baumer et al. 2017;Jacobi, Van Atteveldt, and Welbers 2016;Jacobs and Tschötschel 2019;Nelson et al. 2018).

Scholars working in the area of race and immigration specifically have also begun to adopt NLP techniques as a way to augment traditional qualitative analysis of media sources. For instance, in their study of a mainstream online Swedish internet forum, Törnberg and Törnberg (2016a) found that the discourse related to Muslims on social media was quite similar to the negative portrayals in generalist Western media: Muslims were portrayed as unwelcome immigrants who were "embroiled in conflict," with practices and religious beliefs that conflicted with Swedish values and beliefs. Baker et al. (2008) used NLP techniques to analyze discourse on refugees, asylum seekers, immigrants and migrants (RASIMs) in mainstream British news articles published between 1996-2005. Comparing broadsheets with tabloids, they found differences in the two sources: tabloids were more likely to take negative stances toward RASIMs while broadsheets included negative and positive coverage.

Many authors using NLP techniques to study discourse on race and immigration draw on critical discourse analysis (CDA), and attempt to show how the two approaches can inform each other: "CDA researchers are fundamentally interested in analysing opaque as well as transparent structural relationships of dominance, discrimination, power and control, as they are manifested in language" (Baker et al. 2008:280). Studies integrating CDA into automated text processing analyses typically focus on the "hegemonic" discourse promulgated by the dominant group.

The literature using CDA with automated text analysis to investigate racialized discourses constructed by and directed toward black communities does not cover immigration, but rather focuses on issues that have been identified as central to black communities, such as police profiling, racism, and black activism (Freelon et al. 2018;Jackson and Welles 2016;Rathnayake, Winter, and Buente 2018). These studies often use data from news media and social media outlets such as Twitter, identifying Twitter hashtags such as #blacklivesmatter and #Ferguson as central outlets for "black voices" and perspectives. 2 Several of these papers look at the discourse generated after a young black man -Michael Brown -was killed by police in Ferguson, MO (Freelon et al. 2018;Jackson and Welles 2016). For instance, Jackson and Welles (2016) show how Twitter postings on #Ferguson created a discourse re-affirming black lives and challenging the dominant narratives in the mainstream press. By reframing and critiquing the mainstream discourse displayed in traditional news media, these groups engaged in a "black counter-discourse." The authors used NLP techniques to identify the most frequently re-tweeted comments, and combined this approach with hand-coded content analysis of comments to identify the features of the counter-discourse.

sections.paper_id: Browne et al. - 2022 - Immigration Coverage in the Black Press and the General Audience Press What Can Mixed Methods Revea
sections.container_raw: Overview of Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016)
sections.container_norm: overview of browne deckard and rodriguez 2016
paragraphs: Similar to Jackson and Welles (2016), Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) also apply the theory of black counter-discourse, operationalizing this concept in their comparison of media coverage of immigration in the mainstream and black press along three dimensions: a focus on issues most central to black concerns and history; a valorization of black individuals and black accomplishments; and a critique of white racism. The presence of any of these criteria indicated the presence of a black counter-discourse in their study.

To apply these criteria, the authors hand-coded 73 articles in Atlanta's black press (the Daily World) and 130 articles in Atlanta's general audience press (the Journal-Constitution) published between 2000-2012. The hand-coding procedures identified differences across the two publications, and provided evidence of a black counter-discourse in the Daily World (black press). First, the immigration topics in the Daily World reflected issues most central to black concerns, namely, civil rights, the labor market, and the criminal justice system. One of the most striking findings from the study involved the criminal justice frame. While the majority of the Daily World references to criminal justice focused on concerns about police profiling in minority communities, the majority of Journal-Constitution references to criminal justice focused on immigrants as criminals. None of the Daily World articles depicted immigrants as criminals or a threat to law-and-order.

In addition to police profiling, Daily World articles framed immigration around themes that the literature shows are central to African-American attitudes toward immigration, particularly civil rights and the labor market (Hero and Preuhs 2013). For instance, in a guest column that invites readers to march on Washington to protest Arizona's immigration bill, SB1070, Reverend Al Sharpton emphasizes, With ridiculous state laws like Arizona's anti-immigration SB 1070 bill, Latinos and other minorities are welcoming federal intervention to fully realize Dr. King's dream (Daily World 8/ 23/2010, cited in Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez 2016:9). This passage contains all three criteria for a black counter-discourse. Sharpton implies that African-Americans are part of a larger, deserving group of "minorities," placing black concerns as central and validating those concerns with a reference to Dr. Martin Luther King. The passage also carries a critique of white racism by juxtaposing the need for intervention to "fully realize King's dream" while characterizing Arizona's law as "ridiculous." This passage also emphasizes commonalities between blacks and Latinos.
In contrast to this "commonalities" rendering, columnist Earl Hutchinson invokes a competition frame, and implies that Latinos (immigrants) are taking jobs from African-Americans:

The young black man hesitated as he stood outside the small furniture manufacturing shop in South Los Angeles. He was dressed neatly, and he was well groomed. He eyed the building warily. The sign on the narrow glass door in English and Spanish, read "help wanted" and "trabajo aqui". . . . [He entered and] asked if there would be an interview, [the existing employee] haltingly said only if there was a position open. The young man looked perplexed, glanced at the help wanted sign, politely thanked her and left. A couple of hours later two other young Latinos came in to apply. One was immediately hired . . . the workers in the shop, as they were in nearly all the other shops in the area were Latinos, a large percentage of whom were illegal immigrants . . . . (Hutchinson, Earl Ofari. 2006. "For Latinos Only." Daily World, 4/6. Cited in Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez 2016:13).

This quote also captures criteria for a black counter-discourse from the perspective of blackimmigrant competition. The central figure in the story is a young black man and the main topic is an issue high on the black political agenda -employment. The story depicts antiblack discrimination by describing how a black applicant is not hired but a Latino worker is, explaining that it is typical for jobs to be filled by undocumented Latino immigrant workers rather than blacks.

The two examples also show that a black counter-discourse appeared in articles expressing opposite positions vis-a-vis immigration. That is, a black counter-discourse was present in articles that were supportive of immigrants and immigrant rights, and articles that emphasized black-immigrant competition.

paragraphs: To construct their corpus, Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) searched in Lexis/Nexis for articles with the stemmed term, "immigr*" in the headline or body of the article from 2000-2012. 3 They originally identified 82 articles from the Daily World. The first round of coding showed that nine of the articles did not have any discourse related to the immigration codes or were not focused on immigrants/immigration, so they were dropped from further analysis. Their final sample included the remaining 73 immigration-related articles from this search.

To create a sample of Journal-Constitution that is comparable in size to the Daily World sample, they proceeded through the following steps: they searched for all articles in the Journal-Constitution that covered immigration, using the search stem immigr*; they divided these articles into news stories and op-eds, since the discourse in each of these was quite distinct; and they drew a random sample of five news stories and five op-ed stories for each year from 2000-2012. Their final Journal-Constitution sample consisted of 130 articles.

sections.container_raw: Overview of Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016)
sections.container_norm: overview of browne deckard and rodriguez 2016
paragraphs: To conduct the "hand-coding," Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) used a mixed deductive and inductive iterative method. First, they identified four general frames that were most prominent in the literature on media coverage of immigration: Crime, Racial, Legal, Economic. Next, they conducted a line-by-line coding of the corpus for all themes related to immigration. They then categorized these themes in relation to the four frames, creating sub-codes. For instance, within the "Crime" frame, the following sub-codes emerged: "crime;" "criminals;" "profiling;" "terrorism." Themes that could not be categorized within the four frames, such as education, were subsequently dropped from the analysis. Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) then conducted more in-depth coding of the text segments for evidence of a "black counter-discourse" applying their three criteria (a focus on issues most central to black concerns and history; a valorization of black individuals and black accomplishments; and a critique of white racism.) They used the codes for the sub-themes as indicators of issues that were of concern to blacks. To apply the second and third criteria, they engaged in in-depth qualitative coding that entailed interpretation of the meanings contained within specific passages.

sections.paper_id: Browne et al. - 2022 - Immigration Coverage in the Black Press and the General Audience Press What Can Mixed Methods Revea
Browne et al. - 2022 - Immigration Coverage in the Black Press and the General Audience Press What Can Mixed Methods Revea
paragraphs: Our corpus includes 83 Daily World articles and 130 Journal-Constitution articles, encompassing the full Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) corpus. 4 To prepare our corpus for NLP analysis, we manually identified and stripped header and footer information for each of our 213 articles due to the lack of uniform formatting. We then proceeded with preprocessing the text by converting to lowercase; removing punctuation, stop words, and numbers; and stemming to word roots. We also trimmed the vocabulary by removing any word that only appears in one document. These words inhibit understanding of crossdocument trends and are routinely removed in preparation for topic modeling (Denny and Spirling 2018). Our final corpus consisted of 213 documents and 43,219 word tokens.

paragraphs: Our analysis proceeded in five stages. First, we conducted a simple keyword analysis using theoretically derived dictionaries based on Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) original four-frame analysis: racial, legal, economic, and crime. These dictionaries allowed us to extract word frequencies for each article which could be averaged for each newspaper to produce a simple measure of how each newspaper engages each of the four frames.

Second, we augmented this analysis with a more sophisticated approach using structural topic modeling (STM). Topic modeling is an inductive and iterative machine-learning algorithm that identifies groups of words -or "topics" -that commonly co-occur in a body of text data (Jacobi, Van Atteveldt, and Welbers 2016;Lucas et al. 2015;Mohr and Bogdanov 2013). That is, "topics are defined as distributions over a vocabulary of words that represent semantically interpretable 'themes'" (Roberts et al. 2014(Roberts et al. :1066)). The algorithm identifies latent structures in the data -akin to exploratory factor analysis or principal components analysis -which consist of groups of words that co-occur with high frequency, or "topics." STM builds on Blei and Lafferty's early work on Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) and its dynamic variants (Blei 2012;Blei and Lafferty 2006;Roberts, Stewart, and Tingley 2019). The main contribution of STM over LDA is the ability to account for document-level meta-data variables to facilitate better estimations of topics and draw substantive conclusions (Roberts, Stewart, and Tingley 2019;Roberts et al. 2014). In other words, while LDA identifies "topics" in a text corpus, STM allows the researcher to examine how these topics are related to document metadata. In the present analysis, we employ this functionality to allow topic prevalence to vary by news publication (Journal-Constitution or Daily World). Substantively, this provides a means to apply field theory by 1) allowing the model to use news publications as our indicator of the structural conditions of production when estimating the optimal topic distribution, and 2) allowing us to determine which topics vary significantly by news source and which appear at similar rates in both news sources.

While there is no "right answer" to the number of topics used to analyze a corpus (Grimmer and Stewart 2013), Roberts, Stewart, and Tingley (2019) identify a suite of approximate "goodness of fit" measures for a range of possible topic values. These measures include held-out likelihood (Wallach et al. 2009), residual analysis (Taddy 2012), semantic coherence (Mimno et al. 2011), and topic exclusivity (Airoldi and Bischof 2016). In addition, Mimno and Lee (2014) have developed an algorithm that calculates the number of topics after projecting a word co-occurrence matrix into low-dimensional space and solving for the convex hull. While not the "true" number of topics in a corpus, this allows a data-driven starting point from which to examine the potential number of topics. As Jacobi, Van Atteveldt, and Welbers (2016:95) note:

However, having the 'right' number of topics in a mathematical sense does not say anything about the interpretability of the topics produced. In fact, mathematical goodness-of-fit measures and human interpretation may lead to contradicting conclusions on the best number of topics, given that especially with a high number of topics, the computer algorithm may find nuances that are not semantically meaningful to humans.

Based on examination of these preliminary results, we tested a range of topic numbers from 1-30, evaluating each on a range of goodness-of-fit measures. We found a 15-topic model to be the best fit, maximizing semantic coherence and human-readability. Table 1 provides the top-15 words (beta) for our final 15-topic model.

Importantly, the topic model does not assign labels to identified topics. Instead, the research team must create and assign topic names. To do this, we recruited a team of coders (including two of the authors and four graduate students) to complete two tasks: 1) create new labels for the 15-topic STM results (listed as "new labels" in Tables 1 and 2) assign the four qualitative labels from Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) to the 15-topic STM results. by a Welch's T-test (Kilgarriff 2005;Lijffijt et al. 2016). We find statistically significant differences in the four frames that were identified in the hand-coding: Crime, Racial, Legal, and Economic. Similar to the hand-coding, the dictionary method detects a higher prevalence of issues and perspectives that center on black interests and experiences in the Daily World (Hero and Preuhs 2013). The prevalent terms in the Journal-Constitution more often reflect an anti-immigrant "status quo."

The differences in the usage of race-related terms across the two publications reflect distinct foci stemming from their different positions in the journalistic field. For instance, the Daily World not only uses terms in the "racial" frame more often than the Journal-Constitution, but the most prominent Daily-World terms relate directly to African Americans: "Black," "Africa." The terms, "Latin," "Hispanic," "Race," "Haitian," and "Caribbean" in the racial frame also appear more frequently in the Daily World. To summarize, in Daily World articles on immigration, black populations appear in relation to geographic regions and Latinos are designated by panethnic labels. In the Journal-Constitution, terms associated with race are much less prevalent and immigration is not associated with blacks or African Americans at all. The one term in the racial frame that appears more frequently in the Journal-Constitution is "African."

The crime frame also reflects differences between the two publications consistent with our criteria for a black counter discourse: issues relevant to blacks appear in the Daily World and discourses maintaining the status quo appear in the Journal-Constitution. For instance, even though the term "crime" appears in approximately the same number of articles in the Daily World and Journal-Constitution, a more refined analysis shows that the two publications focus on very different aspects of crime. Paralleling the results in the hand-coding, the dictionary analysis shows that the Daily World is significantly more likely to discuss "profiling," which positions police as threats, while the Journal-Constitution is more likely to use the terms "criminals" and "arrest", which position immigrants as threats.

Finally, the legal frame provides some of the clearest evidence that the Daily World reflects a black counter-discourse and the Journal-Constitution reflects an anti-immigrant discourse. In contrast to the Journal-Constitution, the Daily World is much more likely to include terms associated with Civil Rights (e.g., "King," "Civil Rights") compared to the Journal-Constitution. In stark contrast, the Journal-Constitution uses the term, "illegal" much more often than the Daily World.

In fact, as Figure 1 shows, the largest differences in the terms used to discuss immigration in the four frames are: "black" (Daily World) and "illegal" (Journal-Constitution). These results are consistent with the results of Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) previous hand-coding. When considered through the lens of field theory, these findings point to how structural conditions in the journalistic field can lead news outlets to produce very different news content on the same core issues.

Finally, we note that the two publications contain words associated with the economic frame at approximately the same rate. Employment is an issue at the forefront of political concerns for all racial groups (Hero and Preuhs 2013). The one difference we find for the economic frame is that "employer" appears more often in the Journal-Constitution. A review of the articles that contain the word "employer" shows that these articles most often discuss employers in relation to immigration legislation, and the Journal-Constitution covers immigration legislation with greater frequency than the Daily World.

Overall, our dictionary analyses of the Daily World provide clear and strong evidence for two of the three criteria that Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) use to operationalize a "black counter-discourse:" a focus on issues that are relevant to black readers and the validation of black experiences and individuals. Our NLP analyses provide more limited evidence for the third criterion -a critique of racism. This third criterion requires the more nuanced analysis of structural topic modeling.

Paralleling the results in the hand-coding, the dictionary analysis shows that the Daily World is significantly more likely to discuss "profiling," which positions police as threats, while the Journal-Constitution is more likely to use the terms "criminals" and "arrest", which position immigrants as threats.

Finally, the legal frame provides some of the clearest evidence that the Daily World reflects a black counter-discourse and the Journal-Constitution reflects an anti-immigrant discourse. In contrast to the Journal-Constitution, the Daily World is much more likely to include terms associated with Civil Rights (e.g., "King," "Civil Rights") compared to the Journal-Constitution. In stark contrast, the Journal-Constitution uses the term, "illegal" much more often than the Daily World.

In fact, as Figure 1 shows, the largest differences in the terms used to discuss immigration in the four frames are: "black" (Daily World) and "illegal" (Journal-Constitution). These results are consistent with the results of Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) previous hand-coding. When considered through the lens of field theory, these findings point to how structural conditions in the journalistic field can lead news outlets to produce very different news content on the same core issues.

Finally, we note that the two publications contain words associated with the economic frame at approximately the same rate. Employment is an issue at the forefront of political concerns for all racial groups (Hero and Preuhs 2013). The one difference we find for the economic frame is that "employer" appears more often in the Journal-Constitution. A review of the articles that contain the word "employer" shows that these articles most often discuss employers in relation to immigration legislation, and the Journal-Constitution covers immigration legislation with greater frequency than the Daily World.

Overall, our dictionary analyses of the Daily World provide clear and strong evidence for two of the three criteria that Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) use to operationalize a "black counter-discourse:" a focus on issues that are relevant to black readers and the validation of black experiences and individuals. Our NLP analyses provide more limited evidence for the third criterion -a critique of racism. This third criterion requires the more nuanced analysis of structural topic modeling.
sections.preview_head: Our analysis proceeded in five stages. First, we conducted a simple keyword analysis using theoretically derived dictionaries based on Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) original four-frame analysis: racial, legal, economic, and crime. These dictionaries allowed us to extract word frequencies for each article which could be averaged for each newspaper to produce a simple measure of how each newspaper engages each of the four frames.

sections.paper_id: Browne et al. - 2022 - Immigration Coverage in the Black Press and the General Audience Press What Can Mixed Methods Revea
paragraphs: While the dictionary analysis provides information on the prevalence of specific words, the topic models focus on combinations of these words -that is, topics. Structural topic models allow us to include measures of organizational features -that field theory predicts will affect media content -and to treat them as independent variables. In our study, the key "organizational" feature we use is the type of press (mainstream and black press). We ran regressions to compare the prevalence of the topics in the Daily World and the Journal-Constitution, and report the results in Figure 2. Topics that occur more frequently in the Daily World are: "hate crimes" (Topic 4, see Table 1) and "Civil Rights" (Topic 6, Table 1). Both of these topics reference racial inequality; "hate crimes" are directed toward blacks and immigrants, and "Civil Rights" reference an ongoing struggle for racial equality. In contrast, one of the topics that occurs significantly more frequently in the Journal-Constitution is "national security" (Topic 12), a theme that often casts immigrants as threats (Chavez 2013). The other topics that are more prevalent in the Journal-Constitution than the Daily World encompass seemingly broad, politically neutral themes ("politics," "education," and "legislation"). Note, however, that the word, "illegal" appears in the top five terms in each of these topics (Topic 9,Topic 11,and Topic 14). This is consistent with our dictionary results and supports claims that the general audience press frequently frames immigrants as "unwelcome outsiders" (Chavez 2013).

The STM allows us to quantify the differences in topic prevalence across publications. For example, results reported in Figure 2 show that publication in the Journal-Constitution produces a statistically significant (p < 0.001) proportional decrease of "hate crimes" (Topic 4) by 0.110. 5 Additionally, given that a document is in the Journal-Constitution, we can expect a 0.173 decrease in the proportional discussion of "Civil Rights" (Topic 6, p < 0.001). Furthermore, we expect discussion of "politics" (Topic 9) to increase by 0.099 for articles in the Journal-Constitution compared to those in Daily World (p < 0.01). As field theory predicts, the newspaper content (operationalized here as topics) varies by the two news outlets in ways that are consistent with their two positions in the journalistic field and their experiences with racism and exclusion. The STM also provides statistical validation of common topics across the two media sources. These include the topics of education, demographics, and the court system. Similarities across the Journal-Constitution and the Daily World also include the topic of race. However, when we turn to the hand-coding, we find nuances across the two sources in the discursive construction of race and immigration.

sections.header_raw: Comparing Hand-coding with Topic Modeling: Quantitative ROC Analysis
sections.header_norm: comparing hand coding with topic modeling quantitative roc analysis
paragraphs: Our final step is to conduct more in-depth comparisons of the structural topic modeling results and Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez's (2016) hand-coding. We make this comparison using both an NLP and a qualitative approach. To compare the STM topics with the hand-coding, we calculated the area under the curve (AUC) for the ROC-plot of each combination of topic and hand-coding label pair. Table 2 presents these measures for selected labels (to match the original TSQ paper), with AUC's greater than 0.6 starred.

As we can see, many of the topics from our model match up with the original hand coding labels, suggesting that the topic modeling and hand-coding are distinguishing similar frames in the corpus. This pattern holds for three of the four frames: the crime, legal, and racial frames. For instance, a significant number of the articles that Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) coded with a "crime" frame are prevalent in the "criminal justice" topics -criminal justice, immigration legislation, and court system. A similar pattern occurs for many of the other codes. Articles coded with the "terrorism" label are prevalent in the "national security" topic; articles coded with the "Civil Rights" label are prevalent in the "Civil Rights" topic; articles coded with race subcategories (e.g., Africa, Haiti, Spanish, Latino, Hispanic) are prevalent in the "race" topic; and articles coded with the "Spanish, Latino, Hispanic" label are prevalent in the "demographics" topic.

Unlike the crime, legal, and racial frames, the "economics" frame in the hand-coding does not mirror the topic labels. However, looking at these two approaches together can give us potential insight into the topics. For instance, articles that fall within the employment-jobs and workers frames in the hand-coding fall within the topic "immigration legislation" in the topic modeling. This parallels our finding that the employment-related articles often cover immigration legislation affecting workers and employers.

sections.paper_id: Browne et al. - 2022 - Immigration Coverage in the Black Press and the General Audience Press What Can Mixed Methods Revea
sections.header_raw: Comparing Hand-coding with STM: Qualitative Analysis
sections.header_norm: comparing hand coding with stm qualitative analysis
paragraphs: Finally, to explore differences between STM and hand-coding, we take a "deep dive" into articles that Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) identify as containing particularly clear examples of a black counter-discourse. For instance, consider the article covering the debate over Elian Gonzalez, a six-year-old boy whose mother died at sea when she was bringing him from Cuba to the United States: I am just as intrigued by the less obvious and therefore less remarked upon prejudices that swirl around the Elian Gonzalez case. First and foremost of these is race. Every year, darker skinned children arrive on these shores, fleeing famine and plague in Africa, political oppression in Central America, ethnic persecution in Asia. Yet, we ship those children back without blinking. It is always tempting to blame our own apathy. In truth, the African-American community in particular constantly lobbies on behalf of our Third World cousins. We are flat-out outvoted.

(Hall, Wiley. "Urban Rhythms: Hidden Agenda Hurting Cuban Child," Daily World 1/16/2000, cited in Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez 2016:10) The topic modeling results indicate that 99% of this article falls within Topic 1 -"Race." The most frequent words comprising Topic 1 contains many of the key terms in the passage, including "black," "child," and "Elian." In Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) qualitative analysis, this article was coded as falling within the "race" frame. Notably, while STM correctly identified this article and topic 1 as dealing with race broadly, the qualitative coding detected the use of these terms as part of a broader black counter-discourse that the topic modeling failed to identify.
Our second example also contains a critique of racism with a reference to Dr. Martin Luther King:

Let's face it: There is an eagerness on the part of many to focus on the growing Hispanic population and ignore the long-standing needs that blacks are entitled to. It is our job, however, to stand up and be counted. This is not about being the flavor of the month, but, as Dr. Martin Luther King put it, collecting on the bounced check, marked insufficient funds, that our nation has given its black citizens (Curry, "Another Day of Absence," Daily World 5/4/2006, cited in Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez 2016:12).

The gamma statistic indicates that this article contains high amounts of a single topic: Education (Topic 3, gamma = 24%). In the hand-coding, this article was coded as deploying "race" and "legal" (civil rights) frames, and expressing a "black counter-discourse." Comparing just the label with the hand codes, this seems like very disparate codings. However, the specific terms comprising Topic 3 in the STM show references to "martin," "luther," "king," and "dream." This is an example of how the topic modeling produces "bags of words" rather than meaningful contextualized phrases. Further, in this passage, the term "insufficient" and "entitled" carry substantial weight in shaping the meaning of the passage, and signal a critique of the U.S. racial system. This aspect of the text is lost in the topic model -the terms, "entitled" and "insufficient" do not appear in the top 50 "bag of words" comprising any topic (see Table 2).

As a third example, we compare topic modeling results with another Daily World article that was coded as capturing a "legal" frame as well as a "race" frame by Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) hand coding. The headline for the article is, "Immigration Law in Arizona Reminiscent of King Holiday Repeal."

On Friday, April 23, [Arizona Governor Janet] Brewer signed what is called the most stringent anti-immigration bill in the country. They have likened it to the South African apartheid rule when blacks were required to carry proper papers to justify them to be in certain areas or be arrested, beaten or worse" (Galloway 2010, cited in Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez 2016:17).

The topic modeling results indicate that this article contains multiple topics; fifty-four percent of the article contains Topic 8 -"court systems." The article also contains approximately 15% of the topics, "immigration legislation" (Topic 14), "civil rights" (Topic 6). and "education" (Topic 3), respectively. For this article, the topic modeling discerns finer aspects of the legal frame compared to the results reported in the hand-coding. While this article does not appear to contain Topic 1 -"race" -it does contain 14% of the topic labeled "education" (Topic 3). This corresponds to the terms connected to Dr. Martin Luther King in the top 50 words comprising this topic.

The connection of the topic of immigration with "civil rights" fits within the operational definition of a black counter-discourse. However, the NLP analysis did not capture the implicit critique of racism contained in the comparison between Arizona's immigration law and black suppression under South African apartheid. This example illustrates a limitation to NLP techniques that equate word prevalence with significance. Here, just three short words, "South African apartheid," carry a significant amount of meaning. Further, other terms that carry strong weight in framing the article and denote a critique of racism (blacks "arrested, beaten, or worse") are not included in any of the four topics that are most prevalent in the article. Indeed, the term "beaten" (beat*) is not among the top 50 "bag of words" comprising any topic. Overall, these deeper qualitative readings uncover important meaning occasionally missed in the topic modeling approach -namely, critiques of the U.S. racial system that stem from holding a different, and historically excluded, position in the journalistic field vis-à-vis the mainstream media.

They have likened it to the South African apartheid rule when blacks were required to carry proper papers to justify them to be in certain areas or be arrested, beaten or worse" (Galloway 2010, cited in Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez 2016:17).

The topic modeling results indicate that this article contains multiple topics; fifty-four percent of the article contains Topic 8 -"court systems." The article also contains approximately 15% of the topics, "immigration legislation" (Topic 14), "civil rights" (Topic 6). and "education" (Topic 3), respectively. For this article, the topic modeling discerns finer aspects of the legal frame compared to the results reported in the hand-coding. While this article does not appear to contain Topic 1 -"race" -it does contain 14% of the topic labeled "education" (Topic 3). This corresponds to the terms connected to Dr. Martin Luther King in the top 50 words comprising this topic.

The connection of the topic of immigration with "civil rights" fits within the operational definition of a black counter-discourse. However, the NLP analysis did not capture the implicit critique of racism contained in the comparison between Arizona's immigration law and black suppression under South African apartheid. This example illustrates a limitation to NLP techniques that equate word prevalence with significance. Here, just three short words, "South African apartheid," carry a significant amount of meaning. Further, other terms that carry strong weight in framing the article and denote a critique of racism (blacks "arrested, beaten, or worse") are not included in any of the four topics that are most prevalent in the article. Indeed, the term "beaten" (beat*) is not among the top 50 "bag of words" comprising any topic. Overall, these deeper qualitative readings uncover important meaning occasionally missed in the topic modeling approach -namely, critiques of the U.S. racial system that stem from holding a different, and historically excluded, position in the journalistic field vis-à-vis the mainstream media.
sections.preview_head: Finally, to explore differences between STM and hand-coding, we take a "deep dive" into articles that Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) identify as containing particularly clear examples of a black counter-discourse. For instance, consider the article covering the debate over Elian Gonzalez, a six-year-old boy whose mother died at sea when she was bringing him from Cuba to the United States: I am just as intrigued by the less obvious and therefore less remarked upon prejudices that swirl around the Elian Gonzalez case. First and foremost of these is race.

sections.paper_id: Browne et al. - 2022 - Immigration Coverage in the Black Press and the General Audience Press What Can Mixed Methods Revea
paragraphs: In this paper, we address two goals. First, we apply field theory to the study of media discourse on race and immigration. Second, we assess the benefits and drawbacks of using NLP versus hand coding for studying race and immigration. Theoretically, our approach and findings contribute to the literature by applying field theory to an overlooked arenathe place of black press within the journalistic field. We argue that field theory best explains these patterns of similarities and differences across the two outlets and are due to competition over resources, pressures from the economic and political fields, journalistic principles and practices, and distinct audiences.

On the one hand, the two publications share a need for revenue and an imperative to expand readership to capture the 34% of the Atlanta population that identifies as black (Ruggles et al. 2021). This overlap in goals and competition for resources create similarities in immigration-related topics across both presses. In our NLP analysis, 9/15 topics and 25/ 50 keywords did not have statistically significant differences across the Journal-Constitution and the Daily World. This ability to quantify and statistically test journalistic similarities demonstrates one of the benefits of NLP for field theory.

On the other hand, field theory also explains the differences in journalistic coverage detected by both the NLP and qualitative coding. In our analyses, 6/15 topics and 25/50 keywords had statistically significant differences between the black and mainstream press. This further demonstrates the utility of using NLP to quantify differences in the journalistic field. Rohlinger (2007) contends that the intended audience and reporting style differ in mainstream and specialist outlets. We demonstrate that the distinctive "reporting style" in the black press reflects a "black counter-discourse." Consistent with field theory, we attribute the black counter-discourse to a distinct racially-inflected logic in the black press that includes a mission to promote Civil Rights and provide a counternarrative on many topics (including immigration) to the discourse in the mainstream press.

These counter-narratives are vital for deliberative democracy, as they present viewpoints, ideas, and actors that are absent in the mainstream press (Habermas 1996;Rohlinger 2007). If we take the mainstream media as a stand-in for the "public sphere" envisioned by Habermas, our results suggest it is dominated by perspectives that favor white audiences (Fraser 1990). This underscores the importance of the black press in fully constituting a public sphere conducive to deliberative democracy.

Methodologically, we used two NLP techniques -dictionary analysis and STM -on Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) original corpus of two Atlanta-based newspapers to assess the benefits and drawbacks of each method in comparison with qualitative hand-coding. Both NLP techniques replicate many of the major findings from the hand-coding. Our dictionary analysis replicated and substantiated key differences in frame use between the two sources such as the Daily World's emphasis on race and black experience and the Journal-Constitution's emphasis on "illegal" immigration. Our STM analysis also replicated many of the original hand-coded themes. In addition, these techniques enhance the qualitative results by providing evidence of statistically significant similarities and differences in the occurrence of specific terms and specific topics across media sources (e.g., "economy" in both outlets; "hate crimes" in the Daily World and "national security" in the Journal-Constitution). STM also identified several topics that the hand-coding did not: "hate crimes" in the Daily World and "national security" in the Journal-Constitution. However, these NLP techniques occasionally missed nuances in language -for example, references of a "bounced check" as a critique of racism -and key historical references (such as a reference to South African apartheid) that significantly alter the meaning of text.

paragraphs: Several limitations of the study should be noted. First, news is increasingly published and disseminated on online forums and social media sites, so we may be missing relevant discourse by focusing on traditional news sources (Törnberg and Törnberg 2016b). However, studies comparing online forums and traditional news sources show a high degree of overlap in topics across these two media (Sayre et al. 2010;Zhao et al. 2011). Some scholars have posited a "feedback loop" in which information and ideas conveyed in traditional news media get "picked up" by online sources, so that social media amplifies what is in the news (Törnberg and Törnberg 2016a). Second, this study draws on local media sources from a single metropolitan area. Our findings may therefore not be generalizable to other locations or to news media outlets that have a wider readership.

sections.paper_id: Browne et al. - 2022 - Immigration Coverage in the Black Press and the General Audience Press What Can Mixed Methods Revea
paragraphs: Our approach and our findings are not just consistent with field theory; they illustrate a new avenue for extending the theory. Future research could apply STM on a range of corpora to test hypotheses about organizational features highlighted in field theory. Our approach also suggests some benefits of the NLP techniques for studying issues related to race. First, our dictionary analyses show that it is possible to detect some evidence of a "black counterdiscourse" using single words. Second, in contrast to the deductive approach of dictionarybased analyses, structural topic modeling represents an inductive technique that can identify more complex patterns of word co-occurrence. We found that this approach can detect additional topics beyond what was initially identified through hand-coding.

Although the STM procedures did find patterns that could indicate a black counterdiscourse, they missed nuances in meaning through which a black counter-discourse emerged as taking a pro-immigrant versus anti-immigrant stance. As Lauer, Brumberger, and Beveridge (2018:391) remark: "hand-coding approaches have the advantage of a high-level contextualized interpretation of the language being used." We present two methods for conducting these checks -first, using content analysis from a close reading of select materials in the corpus, and second, calculating the area under the curve for the ROC-plots of each topic and handcoded-label pair. Overall, our analyses make a contribution to the methodological literature by showing the benefits of using mixed methods to understand race-coded language in a corpus.

Our findings suggest several practical implications for researchers considering mixed methods approaches to textual analysis. First, the replication of the qualitative findings using STM techniques opens the door to using STM on large corpuses that would be considered unwieldy for hand coding. However, we recommend that STM techniques be accompanied by "checks" of close qualitative reading given that STM does not taking into account the syntactic context of words (Lauer, Brumberger, and Beveridge 2018). Second, STM could be used as a fruitful first step for theme exploration and inductive code development in otherwise largely hand-coded work. Third, when hand-coding is the primary method, STM could be used as a "check" on hand-coding in terms of the frames identified and as a formal metric for statistical differences of themes between publication types. Finally, not only do our dictionary analyses and STM reveal that there are systematic, statistical differences between the Journal-Constitution and the Daily World that reflect a dominant discourse and counter-discourse, respectively; these methods estimate the extent of both these differences and the similarities. With a large corpus, this approach could be extended to investigating differences in publications from different cities and to tracking statistically meaningful changes in discourse over time.

Our analyses also show the benefits of a mixed-methods approach to examining how the media constructs race in publications aimed at different audiences. Studies comparing discourse in different media sources containing counter-discourses of marginalized groups would benefit greatly from using mixed methods.

paragraphs: 1. We thank an anonymous reviewer for suggesting that we use our analyses to build on field theory and highlighting the relevance of Rohlinger's work. 2. The term, "black Twitter," broadly refers to "a public group of millions of black users on Twitter "networking, connecting, and engaging with others who have similar concerns, experiences, tastes, and cultural practices" (Daniels 2013:225, cited in Rathnayake, Winter, and Buente 2018). 3. Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) generously shared their data and coding sheets with us, and provided detailed explanations of their methods. 4. Our sample includes 9 articles that were not in Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) Daily World corpus. Four of these had been drawn and excluded by Browne, Deckard, and Rodriguez (2016) after they determined that the articles were not about immigrants or immigration. If these articles are peripheral to immigration issues, then the topic models should detect this (the articles will contribute very little to the 15 topics that the procedure identifies). Five articles were missed by Browne et al. when they drew their original sample. At the time, early editions of the Daily World were on microfiche. We re-drew the sample using an online database.

5. The green highlight in Figure 2 indicates that "hate crimes" appears significantly more frequently in the Daily World. The x-axis shows the amount of decrease or increase in topic prevalence between the two publications.

paragraphs: Irene Browne is associate professor of sociology at Emory University. Her research focuses on the topics of immigration, race, and social class and their intersections. You can contact her at: Dept. of Sociology, Emory University, Atlanta, GA 30322. Email: socib@emory.edu John A. Bernau is a postdoctoral fellow in Digital Scholarship at the Center for the Study of Law and Religion at Emory University. His research focuses on the use and evolution of language, especially in religious contexts, as well as recent developments in computational text analysis.
Katharine Tatum received her PhD in sociology from Emory University in 2021. She currently works as a behavioral scientist at Peraton, a contractor for the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.
Jiao Jieyu received her BA degree at Emory University. She is currently an Emory research fellow.

Irene Browne is associate professor of sociology at Emory University. Her research focuses on the topics of immigration, race, and social class and their intersections. You can contact her at: Dept. of Sociology, Emory University, Atlanta, GA 30322. Email: socib@emory.edu John A. Bernau is a postdoctoral fellow in Digital Scholarship at the Center for the Study of Law and Religion at Emory University. His research focuses on the use and evolution of language, especially in religious contexts, as well as recent developments in computational text analysis.

Katharine Tatum received her PhD in sociology from Emory University in 2021. She currently works as a behavioral scientist at Peraton, a contractor for the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.
